title: Wood Christmas Tree Shelf
summary: A wall-hung shelf shaped like a stepped Christmas tree — bevel-mitered 1x4 tiers narrowing toward a shaped treetop, with a plywood infill panel where a shelf is skipped.
description:
This shelf's tree shape comes entirely from a changing saw bevel, not a jigsaw pattern: the main tree body is cut at a 25° bevel, then the treetop section switches to a 30° bevel — track which bevel setting each part needs before cutting, since mixing them up on even one board throws off the tree's silhouette.

Build from the bottom up, attaching each pair of side pieces and its shelf before moving to the next tier — working upward in order is what keeps the tree's narrowing profile consistent, since each tier's position depends on the one below it already being square and level. The decorative side-support shapes get cut before final attachment, and the plan notes cutting them BEFORE assembly specifically, since a small already-attached piece is much harder to clamp down safely for a decorative cut.

One shelf position gets a 1/4" plywood infill panel instead of a full wood shelf — trace that shelf's outline directly onto the plywood for an exact fit, then cut with a circular saw. **This shelf must be screwed into real wall studs**, ideally through both the bottom and back plywood pieces for the widest possible connection to the wall — a loaded shelf holding ornaments or candles is real weight cantilevered off the wall.

materials.name: 1/4" plywood, cut piece, at least 6 in x 15 in
Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.note: Longest point to longest point, both ends cut at 25° bevel.
Long point to short point, both ends cut at 25° bevel; decorative shape jigsawed before attaching.
Longest point to longest point, both ends cut at 25° bevel.
Longest point to longest point, both ends cut at 25° bevel.
Long point to long point, both ends cut at 30° bevel — change saw bevel setting from the main tree cuts.
Long point to long point, both ends cut at 30° bevel.
Long point to long point, both ends cut at 30° bevel.

cutList.material: 1/4" plywood, cut piece, at least 6 in x 15 in
cutList.note: Trace the corresponding shelf outline directly onto the plywood before cutting.

steps.title: Build the trunk
steps.body: Cut a decorative shape into the trunk supports with a jigsaw before attaching — a small already-attached piece is much harder to clamp safely for this cut. Attach the supports to the trunk (back piece) with 1-1/4" nails and glue, or screws.

steps.title: Add the bottom tier
steps.body: Attach the bottom shelf tier to the supports and trunk with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue.

steps.title: Build up the tiers
steps.body: Working upward, attach each pair of side supports to the shelf below, then the next shelf tier, continuing in order through the main tree body with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue.

steps.title: Build the treetop
steps.body: Switch the saw bevel setting to 30° and cut the treetop tiers and cap. Attach the bottom treetop piece the same way as the previous tiers, then attach the remaining sides to complete the tree.

steps.title: Add the plywood infill shelf
steps.body: Trace the second-to-top shelf's outline directly onto the 1/4" plywood, then cut out with a circular saw. Glue and nail in place with 3/4" brad nails.
materials: 1/4" plywood, cut piece, at least 6 in x 15 in

steps.title: Finish and hang
steps.body: Sand and finish. Add hooks. Attach the project to the wall, preferably screwing through the bottom and back plywood pieces into real wall studs.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

unresolvedImages.alt: Wall-hung shelf shaped like a stepped Christmas tree
